entry.title: Towards Evology: A Market Ecology Agent-Based Model
entry.summary: A workshop paper introducing Evology — an empirically calibrated agent-based model that treats US equity mutual funds as an ecology of competing strategies.
entry.content:
Our paper, Towards Evology: a Market Ecology Agent-Based Model of US Equity Mutual Funds, was presented at the Benchmarks in AI workshop at the ACM International Conference on AI in Finance (ICAIF) 2022.

Read it on arXiv.

It is joint work with Aymeric Vié, Alissa M. Kleinnijenhuis, and J. Doyne Farmer. Evology is an empirically calibrated agent-based model that treats the market as an ecosystem of interacting strategies. Where the PNAS paper made the market-ecology argument in a toy model, Evology calibrates the idea to real US equity mutual funds — value and growth styles included — so the interactions between strategies, and their effect on returns, are endogenous rather than assumed.

The model and calibration are in the paper.

entry.title: Evology: an Empirically-Calibrated Market Ecology Agent-Based Model for Trading Strategy Search
entry.summary: A workshop paper using Evology — a calibrated market-ecology agent-based model — as a training environment to search for trading strategies.
entry.content:
Our paper, Evology: an Empirically-Calibrated Market Ecology Agent-Based Model for Trading Strategy Search, was presented at the AI for Agent-Based Modelling workshop at ICML 2022.

Read it.

It is joint work with Aymeric Vié and J. Doyne Farmer. Evology views the market as an ecosystem of diverse, interacting, evolving trading strategies. Because it is empirically calibrated, it doubles as a training environment: rather than assume a strategy and test it in isolation, you let the strategy compete against the live ecology and search for what survives. It is a companion to Towards Evology, which calibrates the same ecology to US equity mutual funds.

The model and the search experiments are in the paper.

entry.title: A Market-Based Approach to Carbon Pricing
entry.summary: A preprint with Yoshua Bengio and colleagues — using retroactive pricing and prediction markets to correct the Social Cost of Carbon.
entry.content:
A new preprint, (Private)-Retroactive Carbon Pricing [(P)ReCaP]: A Market-based Approach for Climate Finance and Risk Assessment, is up on arXiv.

Read the preprint.

It is joint work with Yoshua Bengio, Prateek Gupta, Dylan Radovic, Andrew Williams, Christian Schroeder de Witt, Tianyu Zhang, and Yang Zhang. The Social Cost of Carbon is estimated once and rarely corrected, so today’s prices ignore tomorrow’s evidence. We propose updating it retroactively as that evidence accumulates, letting polluters and insurers settle the difference, and using a prediction market to price the remaining uncertainty.

The mechanism and its analysis are in the paper.

entry.title: How Market Ecology Explains Market Malfunction
entry.summary: Our paper on market ecology is out in PNAS — treating trading strategies as species to explain how markets misprice and destabilise.
entry.content:
Our paper, How Market Ecology Explains Market Malfunction, is now published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

Read it here — it is open access.

It is joint work with Anisoara Calinescu and J. Doyne Farmer. We treat trading strategies as species in an ecosystem: the wealth invested in a strategy is its abundance, and a strategy’s returns depend on how much wealth is crowded into it and its competitors. From that analogy we show how markets misprice assets, amplify shocks, and malfunction — behaviour a representative-agent, equilibrium view does not predict.

The model, the ecology, and the figures are in the paper.

entry.title: Finite-Difference Methods for Option Pricing
entry.summary: Solving the Black-Scholes PDE on a grid. The explicit FTCS scheme and the implicit Crank-Nicolson scheme for a European call, the two spatial grids (S and log S) and why mixing them silently breaks the price, conditional stability, boundary conditions, and convergence to the analytical value.
entry.content:
Adapted from a 2015 computational finance assignment. It joins the Monte Carlo and COS-method posts as a third way to price the same European call.

  • The Equation on a Grid
  • Two Grids, and a Trap
  • The Stencils
  • FTCS: the Explicit Scheme
  • Stability
  • Crank-Nicolson: the Implicit Scheme
  • Boundary Conditions
  • Results
  • Takeaways

The Equation on a Grid

The value V(S,t)V(S,t)V(S,t) of a European option under Black-Scholes obeys

∂V∂t+rS∂V∂S+12σ2S2∂2V∂S2=rV. \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + rS\frac{\partial V}{\partial S} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2 S^2\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2} = rV. ∂t∂V​+rS∂S∂V​+21​σ2S2∂S2∂2V​=rV.

Monte Carlo integrates the terminal payoff over simulated paths; the COS method integrates it against the characteristic function. The finite-difference approach does something more direct: it discretises the equation itself onto a grid in price and time and marches the known payoff at expiry backwards to today. The payoff is the terminal condition, so it is natural to run time in reverse. Write

τ=T−t, \tau = T - t, τ=T−t,

so the payoff sits at τ=0\tau = 0τ=0 and we integrate forward in τ\tauτ. With the usual Greek notation Θ=∂V/∂t\Theta = \partial V/\partial tΘ=∂V/∂t, Δ=∂V/∂S\Delta = \partial V/\partial SΔ=∂V/∂S, Γ=∂2V/∂S2\Gamma = \partial^2 V/\partial S^2Γ=∂2V/∂S2, the equation reads Θ+rSΔ+12σ2S2Γ=rV\Theta + rS\Delta + \tfrac12\sigma^2 S^2\Gamma = rVΘ+rSΔ+21​σ2S2Γ=rV. Everything below prices a European call with K=110K = 110K=110, T=1T = 1T=1, σ=0.30\sigma = 0.30σ=0.30, r=0.04r = 0.04r=0.04, whose analytical value at S0=100S_0 = 100S0​=100 is 9.62549.62549.6254.

Two Grids, and a Trap

Before any discretisation there is a choice of grid, and it is the one place in this procedure that is easiest to get subtly wrong. There are two standard options.

The untransformed grid places nodes at Si=i δSS_i = i\,\delta SSi​=iδS, evenly spaced in price. The PDE keeps its SSS-dependent coefficients rSrSrS and 12σ2S2\tfrac12\sigma^2 S^221​σ2S2.

The log grid changes variables to x=ln⁡Sx = \ln Sx=lnS. Substituting x=ln⁡Sx = \ln Sx=lnS and τ=T−t\tau = T-tτ=T−t into the equation and writing U(x,τ)=V(S,t)U(x,\tau) = V(S,t)U(x,τ)=V(S,t) gives a constant-coefficient equation,

∂U∂τ=12σ2∂2U∂x2+(r−12σ2)∂U∂x−rU. \frac{\partial U}{\partial \tau} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial x^2} + \left(r - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)\frac{\partial U}{\partial x} - rU. ∂τ∂U​=21​σ2∂x2∂2U​+(r−21​σ2)∂x∂U​−rU.

The transformation earns its keep: the diffusion and drift coefficients no longer depend on SSS. Equal steps in xxx are geometric steps in SSS, so the grid is fine where SSS is small and a distant upper boundary costs only a handful of extra nodes.

The coefficients belong to the grid: rSrSrS and 12σ2S2\tfrac12\sigma^2 S^221​σ2S2 on the SSS grid, the constants (r−12σ2)(r - \tfrac12\sigma^2)(r−21​σ2) and 12σ2\tfrac12\sigma^221​σ2 on the log grid. Reaching for the wrong pair is an easy mistake and a surprisingly costly one. The scheme below is built on the SSS grid; the section that follows shows what happens if you borrow the log-grid constants for it.

The Stencils

The three derivatives are replaced by difference quotients from Taylor expansions, and these are the same whichever grid is used. For Θ\ThetaΘ, a forward Euler step in time. Expanding V(S,t−δt)V(S, t - \delta t)V(S,t−δt) about (S,t)(S,t)(S,t),

∂V∂t(S,t)=V(S,t)−V(S,t−δt)δt+O(δt). \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}(S,t) = \frac{V(S,t) - V(S, t-\delta t)}{\delta t} + \mathcal{O}(\delta t). ∂t∂V​(S,t)=δtV(S,t)−V(S,t−δt)​+O(δt).

On the grid, indexing time by τ\tauτ so that a higher index is an earlier calendar time, VinV^n_iVin​ denotes VVV at node iii and step nnn, and this is (Vin−Vin+1)/δt(V^n_i - V^{n+1}_i)/\delta t(Vin​−Vin+1​)/δt.

For Δ\DeltaΔ, a centred difference. The two expansions

V(S±δS,t)=V(S,t)±δS ∂V∂S+12δS2∂2V∂S2+O(δS3) V(S \pm \delta S, t) = V(S,t) \pm \delta S\, \frac{\partial V}{\partial S} + \tfrac12\delta S^2\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2} + \mathcal{O}(\delta S^3) V(S±δS,t)=V(S,t)±δS∂S∂V​+21​δS2∂S2∂2V​+O(δS3)

subtract to cancel the even terms, leaving

∂V∂S(S,t)=Vi+1n−Vi−1n2 δS+O(δS2). \frac{\partial V}{\partial S}(S,t) = \frac{V^n_{i+1} - V^n_{i-1}}{2\,\delta S} + \mathcal{O}(\delta S^2). ∂S∂V​(S,t)=2δSVi+1n​−Vi−1n​​+O(δS2).

Adding the same two expansions cancels the odd terms and gives Γ\GammaΓ,

∂2V∂S2(S,t)=Vi+1n−2Vin+Vi−1nδS2+O(δS2). \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2}(S,t) = \frac{V^n_{i+1} - 2V^n_i + V^n_{i-1}}{\delta S^2} + \mathcal{O}(\delta S^2). ∂S2∂2V​(S,t)=δS2Vi+1n​−2Vin​+Vi−1n​​+O(δS2).

FTCS: the Explicit Scheme

Forward-Time Centred-Space substitutes the three stencils into the PDE and solves for the single unknown Vin+1V^{n+1}_iVin+1​ in terms of three known values at the previous step. On the untransformed SSS grid, because Si=i δSS_i = i\,\delta SSi​=iδS the factor δS\delta SδS cancels against the SSS and S2S^2S2 in the coefficients, and everything collapses to functions of the index iii alone:

Vin+1=aiVi−1n+biVin+ciVi+1n, V^{n+1}_i = a_i V^n_{i-1} + b_i V^n_i + c_i V^n_{i+1}, Vin+1​=ai​Vi−1n​+bi​Vin​+ci​Vi+1n​,

ai=12(σ2i2−ri) δt,bi=1−(σ2i2+r) δt,ci=12(σ2i2+ri) δt. a_i = \tfrac12(\sigma^2 i^2 - r i)\,\delta t,\quad b_i = 1 - (\sigma^2 i^2 + r)\,\delta t,\quad c_i = \tfrac12(\sigma^2 i^2 + r i)\,\delta t. ai​=21​(σ2i2−ri)δt,bi​=1−(σ2i2+r)δt,ci​=21​(σ2i2+ri)δt.

Each new value is read off directly, with no system to solve.

An easy thing to get wrong. The coefficients ai,bi,cia_i, b_i, c_iai​,bi​,ci​ carry the factors σ2i2\sigma^2 i^2σ2i2 and rir iri, which are the SSS-grid coefficients 12σ2Si2\tfrac12\sigma^2 S_i^221​σ2Si2​ and rSirS_irSi​ in disguise. Having just mentioned the log transform, it is tempting to reach instead for its tidy constants (r−12σ2)(r - \tfrac12\sigma^2)(r−21​σ2) and 12σ2\tfrac12\sigma^221​σ2 and drop them in here. That is exactly what I did the first time I coded this up, for the computational-finance course where I first met these methods, and it is worth seeing why it fails so badly. Look at the diffusion term: at S=100S = 100S=100 it is 12σ2S2=0.045×104=450\tfrac12\sigma^2 S^2 = 0.045 \times 10^4 = 45021​σ2S2=0.045×104=450, while the log-grid constant is 12σ2=0.045\tfrac12\sigma^2 = 0.04521​σ2=0.045, smaller by a factor of 10410^4104. Borrow that constant onto the SSS grid and there is almost no diffusion, so value never spreads inward from the in-the-money region and the out-of-the-money call collapses to nothing: the spliced scheme prices the 9.62549.62549.6254 call at 0.00010.00010.0001. The constants are not wrong, they are for the other grid. On the log grid, where the payoff is max⁡(exi−K,0)\max(e^{x_i} - K, 0)max(exi​−K,0) and the step is δx\delta xδx, they are exactly right; on the SSS grid the coefficients must depend on SSS.

Stability

The explicit scheme is only conditionally stable. Its coefficients must stay well behaved, and the binding requirement is bi≥0b_i \ge 0bi​≥0, worst at the top of the grid where iii is largest:

δt≤1σ2imax⁡2+r≈1σ2N2. \delta t \le \frac{1}{\sigma^2 i_{\max}^2 + r} \approx \frac{1}{\sigma^2 N^2}. δt≤σ2imax2​+r1​≈σ2N21​.

The timestep must shrink like 1/N21/N^21/N2. Doubling the asset resolution quadruples the number of timesteps: N=20N = 20N=20 needs 404040 steps, N=100N = 100N=100 needs 100010001000, N=200N = 200N=200 needs 400040004000. The work grows like N3N^3N3, which is the standing weakness of the explicit method and the reason to pay for an implicit one.

Crank-Nicolson: the Implicit Scheme

Crank-Nicolson averages the spatial operator over the old and new time levels. Writing the discretised right-hand side of the PDE as a tridiagonal operator L\mathcal{L}L acting on the value vector, the update is

(I−12δt L)Vn+1=(I+12δt L)Vn. \left(I - \tfrac12\delta t\,\mathcal{L}\right)V^{n+1} = \left(I + \tfrac12\delta t\,\mathcal{L}\right)V^n. (I−21​δtL)Vn+1=(I+21​δtL)Vn.

Now Vn+1V^{n+1}Vn+1 appears on both sides: the three unknowns at each step are coupled, and each step solves a tridiagonal system. That system is cheap, one LU (or Thomas) sweep in O(N)\mathcal{O}(N)O(N), and buys two things. Crank-Nicolson is unconditionally stable, so the timestep is chosen for accuracy rather than survival, and its error is O(δt2+δS2)\mathcal{O}(\delta t^2 + \delta S^2)O(δt2+δS2) against the explicit scheme’s O(δt+δS2)\mathcal{O}(\delta t + \delta S^2)O(δt+δS2). One implementation note worth stating, since it is easy to miss: factor the matrix once, up front, and reuse the factorisation each step. Re-inverting it on every timestep is O(N3)\mathcal{O}(N^3)O(N3) per step and throws the whole advantage away.

Boundary Conditions

The grid is finite, so the scheme needs values at the edges. For a call:

  • At S=0S = 0S=0 the option is worthless, V(0,τ)=0V(0, \tau) = 0V(0,τ)=0.
  • As S→∞S \to \inftyS→∞ the call behaves like the forward, V(Smax⁡,τ)=Smax⁡−Ke−rτV(S_{\max}, \tau) = S_{\max} - K e^{-r\tau}V(Smax​,τ)=Smax​−Ke−rτ: the stock minus the discounted strike. The strike is discounted, not grown; it is easy to write the intrinsic value Smax⁡−KS_{\max} - KSmax​−K and carry it at the risk-free rate by mistake, which has the discounting backwards. An alternative that avoids committing to a value is the linearity condition Γ=0\Gamma = 0Γ=0, imposed as VN=2VN−1−VN−2V_N = 2V_{N-1} - V_{N-2}VN​=2VN−1​−VN−2​.

Even the correct condition is only asymptotic, and imposing it at a finite Smax⁡S_{\max}Smax​ introduces an error, because at a modest boundary the true value still sits below Smax⁡−Ke−rτS_{\max} - K e^{-r\tau}Smax​−Ke−rτ: the option retains a little time value and its delta is not yet one. Pinning the boundary above the true value biases the whole solution up, and refining the grid does not remove it. A boundary placed generously, several times the strike, keeps the effect negligible.

Results

With a generous domain and either scheme, the finite-difference price converges to the analytical value. Convergence is oscillatory: as the grid is refined, the node nearest the spot alternately straddles it from above and below.

At N=100N = 100N=100 both schemes agree with Black-Scholes across moneyness:

S0S_0S0​ analytical FTCS Crank-Nicolson
100 9.6254 9.6250 9.6238
110 15.1286 15.1023 15.1012
120 21.7888 21.7835 21.7842

The grid also gives the Greeks for free, as differences of the value surface it already holds. A centred difference of the solution recovers the delta across the whole range of SSS without any extra solving.

Takeaways

  • Finite differences price an option by discretising the Black-Scholes PDE on a grid and marching the payoff backwards, giving the whole value surface and the Greeks in one solve.
  • The spatial grid is a real choice. Equal steps in SSS keep SSS-dependent coefficients; equal steps in ln⁡S\ln SlnS make them constant and reach a far boundary cheaply. The coefficients belong to the grid, and mixing the two is a silent, severe error.
  • The explicit scheme is simple but conditionally stable, with a timestep shrinking like 1/N21/N^21/N2. Crank-Nicolson removes that constraint for the price of a tridiagonal solve per step and a better order of accuracy.
  • Boundary placement matters as much as grid resolution: too tight a domain caps the accuracy no matter how fine the mesh.

entry.title: Mandelbrot Set: Shallow-Regime Sampling Optimizations
entry.summary: Conservative interior tests for the Mandelbrot set — exact cardioid and period-2 checks plus Monte-Carlo-calibrated period-3 disks — that eliminate ~40% of iteration work with zero false positives.
entry.content:
• The Escape-Time Algorithm and Its Bottleneck
  • Layer 1: Main Cardioid (Exact)
  • Layer 2: Period-2 Disk (Exact)
  • Layer 3: Period-3 Bulbs (Inscribed Disks, MC-Calibrated)
  • Visual Confirmation
  • Conservativeness and Coverage
  • Iteration-Work Savings
  • Design Notes

The Escape-Time Algorithm and Its Bottleneck

The standard escape-time algorithm iterates zn+1=zn2+cz_{n+1} = z_n^2 + czn+1​=zn2​+c starting from z0=0z_0 = 0z0​=0. If ∣zn∣>2|z_n| > 2∣zn​∣>2 for some nnn, the orbit escapes and ccc is colored by the escape iteration count. If the orbit survives to a cap Nmax⁡N_{\max}Nmax​, the pixel is colored black — the point is treated as interior.

Interior points are the expensive case: every one runs to the cap. In a shallow-regime render (full-set views, zoom depths where double precision holds) the typical iteration cap is a few hundred to a few thousand. If a large fraction of pixels land inside the set, those pixels dominate the total work budget.

The leverage is a conservative interior predicate — a cheap test that proves a point is inside:

inside(c)=true  ⟹  c∈M.\texttt{inside}(c) = \text{true} \;\Longrightarrow\; c \in M.inside(c)=true⟹c∈M.

The predicate is allowed to miss interior points (false negatives fall through to escape-time at no extra cost), but must never claim an exterior point is interior (a false positive would mis-color a pixel). This asymmetry is the whole design.

We build three layers, ordered by cost and coverage.

Layer 1: Main Cardioid (Exact)

The main cardioid is the set of ccc for which fc(z)=z2+cf_c(z) = z^2 + cfc​(z)=z2+c has an attracting fixed point. A fixed point satisfies z=z2+cz = z^2 + cz=z2+c with multiplier λ=2z\lambda = 2zλ=2z; attracting means ∣λ∣<1|\lambda| < 1∣λ∣<1. Parametrizing by the multiplier:

c(λ)=λ2−λ24,λ=eiθ.c(\lambda) = \frac{\lambda}{2} - \frac{\lambda^2}{4}, \qquad \lambda = e^{i\theta}.c(λ)=2λ​−4λ2​,λ=eiθ.

To test membership we invert: the fixed point is z=12(1−1−4c)z = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - \sqrt{1 - 4c})z=21​(1−1−4c​) and the interior condition is ∣2z∣<1|2z| < 1∣2z∣<1. The complex square root can be eliminated. With

q=(cx−14)2+cy2,q = \left(c_x - \tfrac{1}{4}\right)^2 + c_y^2,q=(cx​−41​)2+cy2​,

the cardioid interior is exactly the set where

q (q+cx−14)<14 cy2.q\,\bigl(q + c_x - \tfrac{1}{4}\bigr) < \tfrac{1}{4}\,c_y^2.q(q+cx​−41​)<41​cy2​.

This is not an approximation — it is the exact interior. It costs four multiplications and two additions per point.

Layer 2: Period-2 Disk (Exact)

The genuine period-2 points satisfy fc∘2(z)=zf_c^{\circ 2}(z) = zfc∘2​(z)=z. The degree-4 polynomial factors as (z2+z+c+1)(z2−z+c−1)=0(z^2 + z + c + 1)(z^2 - z + c - 1) = 0(z2+z+c+1)(z2−z+c−1)=0, where the first factor gives the 2-cycle after dividing out fixed points. By Vieta’s formulas the product of the two 2-cycle elements is z0z1=c+1z_0 z_1 = c + 1z0​z1​=c+1, and the 2-cycle multiplier is

λ=(fc∘2)′(z0)=4z0z1=4(c+1).\lambda = (f_c^{\circ 2})'(z_0) = 4z_0 z_1 = 4(c+1).λ=(fc∘2​)′(z0​)=4z0​z1​=4(c+1).

Since λ\lambdaλ is linear in ccc, the interior ∣λ∣<1|\lambda| < 1∣λ∣<1 maps to an exact disk:

∣c+1∣<14.|c + 1| < \tfrac{1}{4}.∣c+1∣<41​.

Period 2 is the only nontrivial hyperbolic component whose multiplier is linear in ccc. Every higher-period component has a nonlinear multiplier-to-parameter map, so none of them is an exact disk in the ccc-plane.

Layer 3: Period-3 Bulbs (Inscribed Disks, MC-Calibrated)

The period-3 superattracting parameters — where 000 lies on the 3-cycle, fc∘3(0)=0f_c^{\circ 3}(0) = 0fc∘3​(0)=0 — are roots of

c3+2c2+c+1=0.c^3 + 2c^2 + c + 1 = 0.c3+2c2+c+1=0.

One root is real (c≈−1.7549c \approx -1.7549c≈−1.7549, on the antenna); the conjugate pair (c≈−0.1226±0.7449ic \approx -0.1226 \pm 0.7449ic≈−0.1226±0.7449i) sits on the two round bulbs attached to the main cardioid. The boundaries of these bulbs are not circles — their exact boundary is determined by a degree-9 resultant in ccc — so we inscribe a disk at each center instead.

The inscribed radius must be backed off below the geometric inscribed radius, or the test stops being conservative near the curved boundary. Rather than deriving the exact inscribed radius analytically, we measure a safe lower bound by Monte Carlo:

  1. Draw 1.5 million random samples from the bounding box [−2.2,0.7]×[−1.3,1.3][-2.2, 0.7] \times [-1.3, 1.3][−2.2,0.7]×[−1.3,1.3].
  2. Run escape-time with Nmax⁡=1024N_{\max} = 1024Nmax​=1024 to classify each sample as interior or escaping.
  3. For each period-3 center, record the minimum distance to any escaping sample. This is a stochastic lower bound on the true inscribed radius.
  4. Apply a safety shrink factor SHRINK=0.90\text{SHRINK} = 0.90SHRINK=0.90 to that lower bound.

The antenna center admits only a tiny disk (the filament region is spiky, so escaping points are nearby). The two cardioid-attached bulbs give usable disks:

Center MC lower bound Used radius (×0.90\times 0.90×0.90)
−0.1226+0.7449i-0.1226 + 0.7449i−0.1226+0.7449i ≈0.092\approx 0.092≈0.092 ≈0.083\approx 0.083≈0.083
−0.1226−0.7449i-0.1226 - 0.7449i−0.1226−0.7449i ≈0.092\approx 0.092≈0.092 ≈0.083\approx 0.083≈0.083

The antenna center yields ≈0.008\approx 0.008≈0.008, below the 0.02 threshold used to filter out negligible disks.

Visual Confirmation

The figure below renders the set with log-scaled escape-time coloring and overlays the three analytic regions. The period-3 inscribed disks sit visibly inside their bulbs with margin to spare.

Conservativeness and Coverage

We verify on a fresh 3-million-point random sample (bounding box [−2.2,0.7]×[−1.3,1.3][-2.2, 0.7] \times [-1.3, 1.3][−2.2,0.7]×[−1.3,1.3], Nmax⁡=1024N_{\max} = 1024Nmax​=1024) that no layer produces false positives, then measure interior coverage:

Predicate Interior caught False positives
cardioid ~78% 0
cardioid + period-2 ~91% 0
cardioid + period-2 + period-3 ~94% 0

The cardioid alone captures roughly three-quarters of all interior mass. Adding the period-2 disk crosses 90%. The period-3 disks add a few more percentage points. The long tail of smaller bulbs is deliberately left to escape-time.

If period-3 shows false positives, the shrink factor is too loose — reduce SHRINK until the count reaches zero.

Iteration-Work Savings

Coverage of interior mass understates the true benefit: interior points are exactly the expensive ones, each costing Nmax⁡N_{\max}Nmax​ iterations. We model work explicitly:

  • Baseline: every point costs its escape-time iteration count; interior points cost Nmax⁡N_{\max}Nmax​.
  • With tests: points caught by the predicate cost a small constant (about 4 comparison operations); everyone else is unchanged.

On the 3-million-point sample with Nmax⁡=1024N_{\max} = 1024Nmax​=1024:

reduction=1−work with testsbaseline work≈35–40%.\text{reduction} = 1 - \frac{\text{work with tests}}{\text{baseline work}} \approx 35\text{–}40\%.reduction=1−baseline workwork with tests​≈35–40%.

The exact figure depends on what fraction of the bounding box is interior, which varies with the view. Full-set views have higher interior fraction and therefore larger savings; deep zooms into exterior spirals have near-zero interior fraction and therefore near-zero benefit from these tests.

Design Notes

Why not use larger disks? The period-3 boundary is curved and the inscribed disk underestimates the component. The shrink factor is the margin of safety. A tighter MC sample (more points, higher Nmax⁡N_{\max}Nmax​) converges the lower bound upward, allowing a slightly larger disk, but the gain diminishes quickly.

Why stop at period 3? The inscribable disk radius shrinks roughly as 1/q21/q^21/q2 along the Mandelbrot boundary for period-qqq components, and filament-region components admit almost no inscribed disk at all (the antenna period-3 center above). Past period 3 the per-branch overhead exceeds the iteration savings.

Clean handoff. These tests handle the large round interior components. Everything else — fine filament structures, higher-period bulbs, deep zooms — is handled by escape-time plus periodicity detection (Brent’s cycle detection, or atom-domain tests). The two methods are complementary and compose without interaction.

Deep-zoom regime. In the deep-zoom regime the interior-skipping question becomes secondary to a different bottleneck: double precision no longer resolves the structure, and per-pixel extended-precision arithmetic dominates the budget. The analytic tests remain valid (the cardioid and period-2 disk are exact in exact arithmetic) but the savings they provide are negligible relative to the precision cost.

entry.title: The COS Method for Option Pricing
entry.summary: The Fourier cosine series expansion method for pricing European options. Derives the COS formula from the characteristic function of the log-price process, covers chi and psi payoff coefficients, truncation range selection, and demonstrates convergence under geometric Brownian motion.
entry.content:
• Characteristic Function Approach
  • Chi and Psi Coefficients
  • Truncation Range
  • The COS Pricing Formula
  • Results
    • Price vs Spot
    • Convergence of the Relative Error
    • Value Convergence

Characteristic Function Approach

Under geometric Brownian motion, the log-price increment ln⁡(ST/K)\ln(S_T/K)ln(ST​/K) has a known characteristic function. Rather than working with the probability density directly, we exploit the fact that any density f(x)f(x)f(x) on a finite interval [a,b][a, b][a,b] admits a Fourier cosine expansion

f(x)=2b−a∑k=0∞′Akcos⁡ ⁣(kπ(x−a)b−a), f(x) = \frac{2}{b - a} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}{}' A_k \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi(x - a)}{b - a}\right), f(x)=b−a2​k=0∑∞​′Ak​cos(b−akπ(x−a)​),

where the prime denotes that the first term is halved, and

Ak=∫abf(x)cos⁡ ⁣(kπ(x−a)b−a)dx. A_k = \int_a^b f(x) \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi(x - a)}{b - a}\right) dx. Ak​=∫ab​f(x)cos(b−akπ(x−a)​)dx.

The key insight is that AkA_kAk​ can be recovered from the characteristic function ϕ(u)=E[eiux]\phi(u) = \mathbb{E}[e^{iux}]ϕ(u)=E[eiux] without knowing fff explicitly:

Ak≈Re⁡ ⁣[ϕ ⁣(kπb−a)e−ikπab−a]. A_k \approx \operatorname{Re}\!\left[\phi\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{b - a}\right) e^{-i\frac{k\pi a}{b - a}}\right]. Ak​≈Re[ϕ(b−akπ​)e−ib−akπa​].

For the GBM model, the characteristic function of x=ln⁡(ST/K)x = \ln(S_T/K)x=ln(ST​/K) is

ϕ(u)=exp⁡ ⁣[iu ⁣(r−12σ2) ⁣τ−12σ2τ u2]. \phi(u) = \exp\!\left[iu\!\left(r - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)\!\tau - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\tau\, u^2\right]. ϕ(u)=exp[iu(r−21​σ2)τ−21​σ2τu2].

The cosine expansion converges rapidly for smooth densities. With only a handful of terms, the standard normal density is already well approximated.

Chi and Psi Coefficients

The option price requires computing the inner product of the density coefficients FkF_kFk​ with the payoff coefficients VkV_kVk​. The payoff coefficients decompose into two integrals, χk\chi_kχk​ and ψk\psi_kψk​, that can be evaluated in closed form.

The χk\chi_kχk​ coefficient captures the exponential part of the payoff:

χk(c,d)=∫cdexcos⁡ ⁣(kπ(x−a)b−a)dx=11+uk2[cos⁡(uk(d−a)) ed−cos⁡(uk(c−a)) ec+uksin⁡(uk(d−a)) ed−uksin⁡(uk(c−a)) ec], \chi_k(c, d) = \int_c^d e^x \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi(x - a)}{b - a}\right) dx = \frac{1}{1 + u_k^2}\left[\cos(u_k(d - a))\,e^d - \cos(u_k(c - a))\,e^c + u_k\sin(u_k(d - a))\,e^d - u_k\sin(u_k(c - a))\,e^c\right], χk​(c,d)=∫cd​excos(b−akπ(x−a)​)dx=1+uk2​1​[cos(uk​(d−a))ed−cos(uk​(c−a))ec+uk​sin(uk​(d−a))ed−uk​sin(uk​(c−a))ec],

where uk=kπb−au_k = \frac{k\pi}{b - a}uk​=b−akπ​.

The ψk\psi_kψk​ coefficient captures the constant part:

ψk(c,d)=∫cdcos⁡ ⁣(kπ(x−a)b−a)dx={d−ck=0sin⁡(uk(d−a))−sin⁡(uk(c−a))ukk>0. \psi_k(c, d) = \int_c^d \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi(x - a)}{b - a}\right) dx = \begin{cases} d - c & k = 0 \\ \frac{\sin(u_k(d - a)) - \sin(u_k(c - a))}{u_k} & k > 0. \end{cases} ψk​(c,d)=∫cd​cos(b−akπ(x−a)​)dx={d−cuk​sin(uk​(d−a))−sin(uk​(c−a))​​k=0k>0.​

The integration limits (c,d)(c, d)(c,d) depend on the option type: (0,b)(0, b)(0,b) for calls and (a,0)(a, 0)(a,0) for puts.

Truncation Range

The cosine expansion requires a finite domain [a,b][a, b][a,b]. This range must be wide enough to capture the mass of the density, but not so wide that the cosine basis functions oscillate excessively relative to the number of terms NNN.

Two formulations appear in the literature. The first centres the range on the forward:

a,b=ln⁡(S0/K)+rτ∓Lσ2τ, a, b = \ln(S_0/K) + r\tau \mp L\sqrt{\sigma^2 \tau}, a,b=ln(S0​/K)+rτ∓Lσ2τ​,

and the second uses the first cumulant (the mean of the log-price increment):

a,b=ln⁡(S0/K)+(r−12σ2)τ∓Lσ2τ, a, b = \ln(S_0/K) + \left(r - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)\tau \mp L\sqrt{\sigma^2 \tau}, a,b=ln(S0​/K)+(r−21​σ2)τ∓Lσ2τ​,

where LLL controls the number of standard deviations included. Both formulations converge, but the cumulant-based formula places the interval symmetrically around the true mean of the distribution, which leads to faster convergence at moderate NNN.

The COS Pricing Formula

Combining the density and payoff coefficients, the discounted option price is

V=e−rτ∑k=0N−1′Fk⋅Vk, V = e^{-r\tau} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}{}' F_k \cdot V_k, V=e−rτk=0∑N−1​′Fk​⋅Vk​,

where

Fk=Re⁡ ⁣[ϕ ⁣(kπb−a)eikπb−a(ln⁡(S0/K)−a)] F_k = \operatorname{Re}\!\left[\phi\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{b - a}\right) e^{i\frac{k\pi}{b - a}(\ln(S_0/K) - a)}\right] Fk​=Re[ϕ(b−akπ​)eib−akπ​(ln(S0​/K)−a)]

and

Vk=2b−a K (χk−ψk)(call),Vk=2b−a K (ψk−χk)(put). V_k = \frac{2}{b - a}\, K\, (\chi_k - \psi_k) \quad \text{(call)}, \qquad V_k = \frac{2}{b - a}\, K\, (\psi_k - \chi_k) \quad \text{(put)}. Vk​=b−a2​K(χk​−ψk​)(call),Vk​=b−a2​K(ψk​−χk​)(put).

The computational cost is O(N)\mathcal{O}(N)O(N) per option price: all NNN terms involve elementary trigonometric and exponential evaluations with no matrix inversions or root-finding. This makes the COS method substantially faster than PDE or Monte Carlo methods for European-style contracts.

Results

Price vs Spot

With K=100K = 100K=100, r=0.03r = 0.03r=0.03, T=1T = 1T=1, σ=0.40\sigma = 0.40σ=0.40, the COS price is compared against the analytical Black-Scholes price across a range of spot values. At N=16N = 16N=16 terms, the approximation deviates noticeably for deep out-of-the-money options where the truncation range [a,b][a,b][a,b] (width 2LσT≈9.62L\sigma\sqrt{T} \approx 9.62LσT​≈9.6 with L=12L = 12L=12) demands more basis functions to resolve. At N=64N = 64N=64, the COS price is indistinguishable from the analytical result.

Convergence of the Relative Error

The relative error ∣VCOS−VBS∣/VBS|V_{\mathrm{COS}} - V_{\mathrm{BS}}| / V_{\mathrm{BS}}∣VCOS​−VBS​∣/VBS​ decreases exponentially as the number of terms NNN grows, reaching machine precision around N=65N = 65N=65 for a standard GBM parameterisation (S0=100S_0 = 100S0​=100, K=110K = 110K=110, r=0.04r = 0.04r=0.04, T=1T = 1T=1, σ=0.30\sigma = 0.30σ=0.30).

With L=12L = 12L=12, both truncation formulas converge at nearly the same rate. The difference between their centres (0.5σ2τ0.5\sigma^2\tau0.5σ2τ) is small relative to the total range width (2Lστ2L\sigma\sqrt{\tau}2Lστ​), so the effect of mis-centring is negligible. With a smaller LLL, the cumulant-based formula would show a more pronounced advantage.

Value Convergence

The raw option value oscillates at small NNN but stabilises rapidly. For the at-the-money case (S0=K=100S_0 = K = 100S0​=K=100, r=0.03r = 0.03r=0.03, T=1T = 1T=1, σ=0.40\sigma = 0.40σ=0.40), the oscillations decay and both truncation formulas settle on the analytical value by N≈25N \approx 25N≈25.

entry.title: Monte Carlo Methods for Option Pricing
entry.summary: Monte Carlo simulation for pricing European, digital and Asian options under Black-Scholes dynamics. Covers convergence properties, antithetic variates, bump-and-revalue sensitivity estimation, and control variates for variance reduction.
entry.content:
• European Option Pricing
    • Convergence
    • Antithetic Variates
    • Standard Error vs Moneyness and Volatility
  • Sensitivity Estimation
    • Seed Control
    • European Delta vs Strike
    • Digital Option Delta
    • Smoothed Payoff
  • Variance Reduction for Asian Options
    • Control Variates
    • Asian Option: Standard Error Analysis

European Option Pricing

We price European call options under geometric Brownian motion

dS=rS dt+σS dZ, dS = rS\,dt + \sigma S\,dZ, dS=rSdt+σSdZ,

with parameters S0=100S_0 = 100S0​=100, K=99K = 99K=99, σ=0.2\sigma = 0.2σ=0.2, r=0.06r = 0.06r=0.06, T=1T = 1T=1.

The GBM admits the closed-form solution

ST=S0exp⁡ ⁣[(r−12σ2)T+σT Z], S_T = S_0 \exp\!\left[\left(r - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)T + \sigma\sqrt{T}\,Z\right], ST​=S0​exp[(r−21​σ2)T+σT​Z],

which we discretise over nnn time steps as

St+1=Stexp⁡ ⁣[(r−12σ2)Δt+σΔt ϕ],ϕ∼N(0,1). S_{t+1} = S_t \exp\!\left[\left(r - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)\Delta t + \sigma\sqrt{\Delta t}\,\phi\right], \quad \phi \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1). St+1​=St​exp[(r−21​σ2)Δt+σΔt​ϕ],ϕ∼N(0,1).

The discounted expected payoff e−rT E[max⁡(ST−K,0)]e^{-rT}\,\mathbb{E}[\max(S_T - K, 0)]e−rTE[max(ST​−K,0)] is estimated by averaging over simulated paths. The Black-Scholes analytical price for these parameters is V=11.5443V = 11.5443V=11.5443 with delta Δ=0.6737\Delta = 0.6737Δ=0.6737.

Convergence

Increasing the number of Monte Carlo paths reduces the standard error as O(1/n)\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{n})O(1/n​).

Antithetic Variates

For each random draw ZZZ, we also evaluate the payoff at −Z-Z−Z. Since the two payoffs are negatively correlated, their average has lower variance:

V^=12[f(Z)+f(−Z)]. \hat{V} = \frac{1}{2}\left[f(Z) + f(-Z)\right]. V^=21​[f(Z)+f(−Z)].

Standard Error vs Moneyness and Volatility

The standard error depends on the option’s moneyness and on volatility. Deep in-the-money options have less payoff variance; higher volatility increases it.

Sensitivity Estimation

The hedge parameter Δ=∂V/∂S\Delta = \partial V / \partial SΔ=∂V/∂S is estimated by the bump-and-revalue method:

Δ^=V(S0+ε)−V(S0)ε. \hat{\Delta} = \frac{V(S_0 + \varepsilon) - V(S_0)}{\varepsilon}. Δ^=εV(S0​+ε)−V(S0​)​.

Seed Control

When different random seeds are used for the base and bumped simulations, the noise in each estimate is independent, and the finite-difference ratio becomes dominated by MC noise at small ε\varepsilonε. Using the same seed ensures the same random draws, so the noise cancels in the numerator.

European Delta vs Strike

With the same-seed approach, the MC delta estimate tracks the analytical Black-Scholes delta across the full range of strikes.

Digital Option Delta

A digital (binary) call pays 1 if ST>KS_T > KST​>K and 0 otherwise. The analytical delta is

Δdigital=e−rT n(d2)σ S T, \Delta_{\mathrm{digital}} = \frac{e^{-rT}\,n(d_2)}{\sigma\,S\,\sqrt{T}}, Δdigital​=σST​e−rTn(d2​)​,

where n(⋅)n(\cdot)n(⋅) is the standard normal density. The discontinuous payoff function produces noisy delta estimates via bump-and-revalue.

Smoothed Payoff

Replacing the discontinuous payoff with a logistic approximation 11+e−k(ST−K)\frac{1}{1 + e^{-k(S_T - K)}}1+e−k(ST​−K)1​ produces smoother delta estimates.

Variance Reduction for Asian Options

An Asian call option with arithmetic averaging has payoff max⁡(Aˉ−K,0)\max(\bar{A} - K, 0)max(Aˉ−K,0) where Aˉ=1N∑i=1NSti\bar{A} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} S_{t_i}Aˉ=N1​∑i=1N​Sti​​. No closed-form solution exists for the arithmetic average case.

Control Variates

The geometric-average Asian option A~=(∏i=1NSti)1/N\tilde{A} = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{N} S_{t_i}\right)^{1/N}A~=(∏i=1N​Sti​​)1/N does admit an analytical price:

Vgeo=e−rT[S0 eA+B2/2 N(c+B)−K N(c)], V_{\mathrm{geo}} = e^{-rT}\left[S_0\,e^{A + B^2/2}\,N(c + B) - K\,N(c)\right], Vgeo​=e−rT[S0​eA+B2/2N(c+B)−KN(c)],

where A=(r−σ2/2) T/2A = (r - \sigma^2/2)\,T/2A=(r−σ2/2)T/2, B=σT(2N+1)/(6(N+1))B = \sigma\sqrt{T}\sqrt{(2N+1)/(6(N+1))}B=σT​(2N+1)/(6(N+1))​, and c=(ln⁡(S0/K)+A)/Bc = (\ln(S_0/K) + A)/Bc=(ln(S0​/K)+A)/B.

Using the geometric average payoff as a control variate with optimal β\betaβ:

V^CV=V^arith−β(V^geo−Vgeoanalytical). \hat{V}_{\mathrm{CV}} = \hat{V}_{\mathrm{arith}} - \beta\left(\hat{V}_{\mathrm{geo}} - V_{\mathrm{geo}}^{\mathrm{analytical}}\right). V^CV​=V^arith​−β(V^geo​−Vgeoanalytical​).

Asian Option: Standard Error Analysis

entry.title: Netting and Resolving Debt with Graphs
entry.summary: Modelling a financial system’s debts as a directed graph and simplifying them. Netting through a clearing party, a trust-constrained reduction algorithm, the fixed-cost variant as Subset Sum, and dependent debt as Minimum Cost Feedback Arc Set, with an NP-hardness reduction from Vertex Cover.
entry.content:
Adapted from a chapter of my undergraduate dissertation, “Solving Financial Problems using Algorithmic Graph Theory” (2013).

  • The Problem
  • Compensating Debt
  • Trust Relations
  • Fixed Transaction Costs
  • Dependent Debt
    • Feedback Arc Set is NP-complete
  • Takeaways
  • References

The Problem

Debt is an obligation owed by one party (the debtor) to pay another party (the creditor) some prespecified asset: currency, a product, or an intermediate good. In a financial system many such relations exist at once, and a single entity can be both a debtor to one party and a creditor to another. Unresolved debt has consequences for everyone involved, so we would like to simplify the debt relations as far as possible.

Let VVV be the set of parties, modelled as a directed graph. An edge e(vi,vj)∈Ee(v_i, v_j) \in Ee(vi​,vj​)∈E annotated with dij∈R+d_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}^+dij​∈R+ means viv_ivi​ owes dijd_{ij}dij​ to vjv_jvj​. The edge set EEE is the debt structure.

Two quantities describe each entity. The balance is everything owed to the entity minus everything it owes:

balance(vi)=∑j=1, j≠indji−∑j=1, j≠indij. \mathrm{balance}(v_i) = \sum_{j=1,\,j\neq i}^{n} d_{ji} - \sum_{j=1,\,j\neq i}^{n} d_{ij}. balance(vi​)=j=1,j=i∑n​dji​−j=1,j=i∑n​dij​.

The transferable amount is the value that, when all debts are settled, moves straight through the entity from its debtors to its creditors:

transferable(vi)=min⁡ ⁣(∑j=1, j≠indji,  ∑j=1, j≠indij). \mathrm{transferable}(v_i) = \min\!\left( \sum_{j=1,\,j\neq i}^{n} d_{ji},\; \sum_{j=1,\,j\neq i}^{n} d_{ij} \right). transferable(vi​)=min​j=1,j=i∑n​dji​,j=1,j=i∑n​dij​​.

Simplifying the system means reducing this pass-through: every unit that merely flows through an intermediary is a transaction that did not need to happen.

Compensating Debt

Take the simplest instance: all debts are sums of currency, the denomination is (near) fractional, and every entity accepts a debt from any other party. Build the graph and compute each party’s balance. The balances always sum to zero, because every debt is counted once positively and once negatively:

∑i=1nbalance(vi)=∑i=1n ⁣(∑jdji−∑jdij)=∑i∑jdji−∑i∑jdij=0. \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{balance}(v_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left( \sum_{j} d_{ji} - \sum_{j} d_{ij} \right) = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} d_{ji} - \sum_{i}\sum_{j} d_{ij} = 0. i=1∑n​balance(vi​)=i=1∑n​(j∑​dji​−j∑​dij​)=i∑​j∑​dji​−i∑​j∑​dij​=0.

A party is a net debtor if its balance is below zero and a net creditor if above. Anyone with equal debt and credit has balance zero and drops out of the problem entirely.

This gives an immediate solution. Introduce a neutral party vn+1v_{n+1}vn+1​. For every net debtor add an edge to vn+1v_{n+1}vn+1​ with weight ∣balance(vi)∣|\mathrm{balance}(v_i)|∣balance(vi​)∣; for every net creditor add an edge from vn+1v_{n+1}vn+1​ with weight ∣balance(vi)∣|\mathrm{balance}(v_i)|∣balance(vi​)∣. Remove all other edges. The neutral party collects from the debtors and redistributes to the creditors, and every original party now has exactly one transaction to make.

This is central clearing. A central counterparty novates every bilateral contract, and the whole graph collapses to a star. Much of the over-the-counter derivatives market was pushed onto this structure after 2008, under Dodd-Frank in the United States and EMIR in Europe.

A solution is optimal when no party has both debt and credit, which is to say when the graph has no path of length two or more. We can pose the corresponding decision problem: can the debts be reduced so that ∣E∣≤k|E| \le k∣E∣≤k for some chosen kkk?

Trust Relations

Credit and debt are forms of trust. A creditor may not want to swap a debt owed by a party it does business with for a debt owed by a party it knows nothing about, especially when the debt is a long-term loan, or the unknown party sits in an economically sensitive sector.

Call a debtor viv_ivi​ external to a creditor vjv_jvj​ if there is no path from viv_ivi​ to vjv_jvj​ in the original problem. Now consider the variant where entities refuse debt from parties external to them. The clearing-party trick no longer works: routing everything through a neutral hub can leave a creditor indirectly dependent on an external debtor once the hub redistributes.

We can still reduce the transferable amount without violating trust, by only ever short-circuiting debts that already share an endpoint. For each entity, repeatedly match one of its incoming debts against one of its outgoing debts and route that amount directly from the upstream debtor to the downstream creditor:

function ReduceDebts(G(V, E)):
    for each v in V:
        I ← in-edges of v
        O ← out-edges of v
        while I ≠ ∅ and O ≠ ∅:
            pick e(s, v) ∈ I with weight d_i
            pick e(v, t) ∈ O with weight d_o
            d ← min(d_i, d_o)
            if an edge e(s, t) with weight d_st exists:
                d_st ← d_st + d
            else:
                add edge e(s, t) with weight d
            if d_i = d:
                I ← I − {e(s, v)}
            else:
                O ← O − {e(v, t)}
    return G(V, E)

Because the new edge e(s,t)e(s, t)e(s,t) only connects a party that already reached vvv to one vvv already reached, no new trust relation is created that the original graph did not permit.

Running time. The procedure is in NP, since a solution is verified in polynomial time by checking that the transferable amount of every party is zero, and it runs in O(∣V∣2)\mathrm{O}(|V|^2)O(∣V∣2) time. The outer loop runs exactly ∣V∣|V|∣V∣ times. A vertex has at most ∣V∣−1|V| - 1∣V∣−1 incident edges, and each pass of the inner loop removes at least one of them, so the inner loop runs at most ∣V∣−2|V| - 2∣V∣−2 times. After that either III or OOO is empty, the transferable amount is zero, and the party is optimal. The sets III and OOO only ever shrink, since no debt is created out of nothing, so the bound holds.

ReduceDebts is the decentralised cousin of clearing, known in practice as portfolio compression. There is no central counterparty, only bilateral tear-ups that preserve each party’s net position. The procedure is deliberately local, and its outcome is order-dependent: which debts you short-circuit, and in which order, changes the result, and there is no canonical optimum. Compression services such as TriOptima’s triReduce grew large after 2008 to shrink gross notional under leverage-ratio rules. D’Errico and Roukny (2017) model exactly this as a graph problem.

Fixed Transaction Costs

A different variant: all debts are accepted, but settling one incurs a fixed cost that is independent of the amount, a flat transaction fee. When debtors and creditors do not match one to one, minimising the total cost is no longer trivial, and the question becomes how to settle everyone in the fewest transfers.

This has a clean structural answer. Drop the parties whose balance is already zero. The minimum number of transfers is then n−kn - kn−k, where kkk is the largest number of zero-sum groups the balances partition into. A group of mmm parties settles internally in exactly m−1m - 1m−1 transfers, and independent groups do not interact, so minimising transactions means maximising the number of zero-sum groups. Merely deciding whether you can beat the trivial n−1n - 1n−1, which is to say whether any proper zero-sum subset exists at all, is already the Subset Sum problem, and so NP-complete.

With arbitrary debtor-to-creditor transfers and a flat charge per used edge, this is the fixed-charge transportation problem, a classic NP-hard problem in combinatorial optimisation. The greedy rule that expense-splitting apps use, largest debtor pays largest creditor, is a heuristic with no constant-factor guarantee.

Dependent Debt

The richest variant treats a debt as a semi-finished product or material in a production process. A party is now a producer or purchaser of some commodity, and its transferable amount is the total it must produce in order to pay off its own suppliers.

This invites a failure mode that currency debt does not have. A producer can come to depend, indirectly, on itself: a blocked production line. In the graph this is a directed cycle: v1v_1v1​ needs an input from v2v_2v2​, which needs one from v3v_3v3​, which needs one back from v1v_1v1​. Nothing can start. The deadlock cannot be cleared without some external party investing into the cycle.

The problem becomes: find a set of edges E′⊂EE' \subset EE′⊂E that, when removed, makes the graph acyclic, while minimising the total cost of the removed edges. This is the Minimum Cost Feedback Arc Set, a weighted version of the Feedback Arc Set decision problem (does there exist E′⊂EE' \subset EE′⊂E with ∣E′∣≤k|E'| \le k∣E′∣≤k whose removal makes GGG acyclic?). The minimum-cost version is at least as hard as ordinary Feedback Arc Set; run the cost algorithm on a graph with all edge weights equal to one.

Feedback Arc Set is NP-complete

FAS is in NP. Given an instance G(V,E)G(V, E)G(V,E) and a candidate solution E′E'E′, assert ∣E′∣≤k|E'| \le k∣E′∣≤k, let G′=(V,E−E′)G' = (V, E - E')G′=(V,E−E′), and build a spanning tree of G′G'G′ by depth-first search. If a back-edge is found the graph still has a cycle, so E′E'E′ is not a solution. The certificate is valid iff G′G'G′ is acyclic and ∣E′∣≤k|E'| \le k∣E′∣≤k, which is checkable in polynomial time.

FAS is NP-hard, by reduction from Minimum Vertex Cover, itself a known NP-complete problem. A vertex cover is a set V′⊆VV' \subseteq VV′⊆V such that every edge of GGG touches at least one vertex in V′V'V′; the decision version asks whether one of size ≤K\le K≤K exists.

Given a Vertex Cover instance G(V,E)G(V, E)G(V,E), construct a directed graph G′(V′,E′)G'(V', E')G′(V′,E′):

  • For each vertex vi∈Vv_i \in Vvi​∈V, create two vertices viv_ivi​ and vi′v_i'vi′​ joined by a directed edge e(vi,vi′)e(v_i, v_i')e(vi​,vi′​).
  • For each undirected edge e(vi,vj)∈Ee(v_i, v_j) \in Ee(vi​,vj​)∈E, add the two directed edges e(vj′,vi)e(v_j', v_i)e(vj′​,vi​) and e(vi′,vj)e(v_i', v_j)e(vi′​,vj​).

Every cycle in G′G'G′ must pass through some “internal” edge e(vi,vi′)e(v_i, v_i')e(vi​,vi′​), and there is exactly one such edge per original vertex. Removing e(vi,vi′)e(v_i, v_i')e(vi​,vi′​) corresponds to placing viv_ivi​ in the cover. A set of internal edges breaks all cycles in G′G'G′ if and only if the corresponding vertices cover all edges of GGG, so a minimum Feedback Arc Set in G′G'G′ yields a minimum Vertex Cover in GGG, and the two problems have the same hardness.

Takeaways

  • Pure-currency netting with universal trust is easy: a single clearing party reduces the whole system to one transaction per participant, because balances sum to zero.
  • Add trust constraints and you give up the clearing party, but an O(∣V∣2)\mathrm{O}(|V|^2)O(∣V∣2) pass that only short-circuits already-connected debts still reduces the pass-through without inventing new exposures.
  • The realistic frictions are where it gets hard. Flat transaction fees make the problem Subset Sum (NP-complete); production dependencies make it Minimum Cost Feedback Arc Set (NP-hard).

Each of the three problems in the dissertation followed this shape: write the financial system as a graph, and the question you actually care about turns out to be a named graph problem whose difficulty is already understood. The companion posts do the same for portfolio diversification and currency arbitrage.

References

  • R. M. Karp (1972). Reducibility Among Combinatorial Problems. Vertex Cover, Feedback Arc Set, and Subset Sum (as Knapsack) are among the 21 problems shown NP-complete here.
  • M. D’Errico and T. Roukny (2017). Compressing Over-the-Counter Markets. Models multilateral portfolio compression as a network problem.

entry.title: Building a Maximally Diversified Portfolio
entry.summary: Turning diversification into a graph problem. Building a correlation graph from stock returns, casting a maximally diversified portfolio as Maximum Independent Set / Maximum Clique, and benchmarking exact Bron-Kerbosch enumeration against the Halldórsson-Boppana approximation on the S&P 500.
entry.content:
Adapted from a chapter of my undergraduate dissertation, “Solving Financial Problems using Algorithmic Graph Theory” (2013).

  • The Problem
  • A Correlation Graph
  • Complexity
    • Exact: Bron-Kerbosch
    • Approximate: Halldórsson-Boppana
  • Dataset and Results
  • How Much of This Is Noise?
  • Takeaways
  • References

The Problem

In the Capital Asset Pricing Model the risk of holding a portfolio splits into two kinds. Systematic risk is the risk shared by every stock in a market, sector, or whole financial system. Unsystematic (or idiosyncratic) risk is specific to one asset. Exposure to unsystematic risk can be reduced by diversifying, and a portfolio is more diversified when the stocks added to it are uncorrelated with the stocks already in it.

So the maximally diversified portfolio problem is: find a large set of stocks that are mutually uncorrelated.

A Correlation Graph

Take NNN stocks and the matrix MMM of pairwise correlations. Fix a maximum accepted correlation ϵ\epsilonϵ. Build a graph GGG whose vertices are the stocks, joining two stocks with an edge whenever their correlation is at most ϵ\epsilonϵ, that is, whenever they are acceptably uncorrelated.

For the correlation itself we use the Pearson correlation of the two discrete-time price signals s1s_1s1​ and s2s_2s2​ over t=0…Tt = 0 \ldots Tt=0…T:

r(s1,s2)=∑tT(s1[t]−μ(s1))(s2[t]−μ(s2))∑tT(s1[t]−μ(s1))2  ∑tT(s2[t]−μ(s2))2. r(s_1, s_2) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{t}^{T} \big(s_1[t] - \mu(s_1)\big)\big(s_2[t] - \mu(s_2)\big)} {\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{t}^{T} \big(s_1[t] - \mu(s_1)\big)^2}\;\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{t}^{T} \big(s_2[t] - \mu(s_2)\big)^2}}. r(s1​,s2​)=t∑T​(s1​[t]−μ(s1​))2​t∑T​(s2​[t]−μ(s2​))2​t∑T​(s1​[t]−μ(s1​))(s2​[t]−μ(s2​))​.

In this graph a valid portfolio is a set of stocks that are all pairwise uncorrelated, a set of vertices that are all mutually adjacent. That is a clique, and the largest such portfolio is a Maximum Clique.

The same problem has a mirror image. Build the complement graph instead, joining two stocks when they are too correlated (∣r∣≥ϵ|r| \ge \epsilon∣r∣≥ϵ). Now a diversified portfolio is a set with no edges between any of its members, a Maximum Independent Set. The two formulations are complementary: a clique in one graph is an independent set in the other, and the complement is generated in O(∣V∣2)\mathrm{O}(|V|^2)O(∣V∣2) time by inverting every entry of MMM.

Complexity

Both Maximum Clique and Maximum Independent Set are NP-hard optimisation problems. A graph can have up to 3∣V∣/33^{|V|/3}3∣V∣/3 maximal cliques, so any method that enumerates them all is exponential in the worst case.

Exact: Bron-Kerbosch

The Bron-Kerbosch algorithm enumerates every maximal clique. It carries three sets: RRR, the clique built so far; PPP, the candidates that extend it; and XXX, vertices already processed.

function BronKerbosch(R, P, X):
    if P = ∅ and X = ∅:
        emit(R)                       # R is a maximal clique
    else:
        for each v in P:
            BronKerbosch(R ∪ {v},
                         P ∩ neighbours(v),
                         X ∩ neighbours(v))
            P ← P − {v}
            X ← X ∪ {v}

The initial call is BronKerbosch(∅, V, ∅). A clique is recorded when there are no candidates left and no already-processed vertices that could extend it. At each step a vertex vvv is added to RRR, the candidate set is narrowed to P∩neighbours(v)P \cap \mathit{neighbours}(v)P∩neighbours(v) so that everything stays mutually connected, and afterwards vvv moves from PPP into XXX. The algorithm enumerates all cliques in time linear in their number, and since that number is exponential it runs in O(3∣V∣/3)\mathrm{O}(3^{|V|/3})O(3∣V∣/3).

Approximate: Halldórsson-Boppana

For thousands of stocks, exhaustive enumeration is hopeless, so we also implement the Halldórsson-Boppana approximation, which approximates the Maximum Independent Set (and by complement the Maximum Clique) within a factor of O ⁣(n/log⁡2n)\mathrm{O}\!\big(n / \log^2 n\big)O(n/log2n). It is built from two routines. Ramsey recursively splits the graph at a vertex into its neighbours and non-neighbours, returning a clique and an independent set:

function Ramsey(G):
    if G = ∅:
        return (∅, ∅)
    choose some v in V
    (C1, I1) ← Ramsey(Neighbours(v))
    (C2, I2) ← Ramsey(NonNeighbours(v))
    return ( largest(C1 ∪ {v}, C2),
             largest(I1, I2 ∪ {v}) )

CliqueRemoval repeatedly strips a clique returned by Ramsey and keeps the best independent set found across the rounds:

function CliqueRemoval(G):
    i ← 1
    (C_i, I_i) ← Ramsey(G)
    while G ≠ ∅:
        G ← G − C_i
        i ← i + 1
        (C_i, I_i) ← Ramsey(G)
    return ( max over j of I_j, {C_1, C_2, … C_i} )

Dataset and Results

The correlations were computed over 475 of the 500 stocks in the S&P 500, from 2013-01-01 to 2013-07-01, using the Pearson function above. The correlation matrix had mean μ=0.306\mu = 0.306μ=0.306 and standard deviation σ=0.144\sigma = 0.144σ=0.144. From it we generated three graphs, at correlation thresholds 0.10.10.1, 0.20.20.2, and 0.30.30.3, with the density of a graph defined as

D(G)=∣E∣∣V∣ (∣V∣−1). D(G) = \frac{|E|}{|V|\,(|V| - 1)}. D(G)=∣V∣(∣V∣−1)∣E∣​.

Because the mean correlation is 0.3060.3060.306, a low threshold admits very few pairs (the graph is sparse), while a threshold near the mean admits roughly half of all pairs. At ϵ=0.3\epsilon = 0.3ϵ=0.3 the graph has ∣E∣=102,922|E| = 102{,}922∣E∣=102,922 edges on ∣V∣=475|V| = 475∣V∣=475 vertices, a density of about 0.460.460.46. For each graph we ran Bron-Kerbosch (recording the largest clique and the time) and the Halldórsson-Boppana approximation (best clique over 10 runs, averaged time).

Threshold ϵ\epsilonϵ Density Bron-Kerbosch size BK time Halldórsson-Boppana size H&B time
0.1 0.08 15 5.4 s 2 6.6 s
0.2 0.26 49 27 min 4 5.8 s
0.3 0.46 62 2 h 5 8.2 s

There is no single best method. Bron-Kerbosch finds large cliques quickly on sparse graphs but becomes infeasible as density grows, taking two hours at ϵ=0.3\epsilon = 0.3ϵ=0.3. The approximation is fast and its runtime barely moves with density, but it returns cliques of size 2 to 5, which is useless here: the literature suggests around 30 stocks are needed to diversify away most idiosyncratic risk. If an investor is restricted to the S&P 500, the only way to obtain a portfolio of usable size is exhaustive enumeration, paid for in computation time.

How Much of This Is Noise?

There is a bridge between this combinatorial picture and the spectral one. Suppose the correlation matrix were pure noise, with its eigenvalues filling the Marchenko-Pastur sea. Thresholding it at ϵ\epsilonϵ would then, to first order, produce an Erdős-Rényi graph G(n,p)G(n, p)G(n,p) with edge probability p=P(∣r∣>ϵ)p = P(|r| > \epsilon)p=P(∣r∣>ϵ) under the null. Erdős-Rényi graphs have a sharply concentrated clique number of about 2ln⁡n/ln⁡(1/p)2 \ln n / \ln(1/p)2lnn/ln(1/p), only logarithmic in nnn. That gives a free null model for the table above. At n=475n = 475n=475 and ϵ=0.3\epsilon = 0.3ϵ=0.3, so p≈0.46p \approx 0.46p≈0.46, noise alone would yield cliques of about 16, where we found 62. A clique that large cannot be explained by noise, so it is reporting genuine correlation structure.

What lifts the real cliques above that noise floor is the handful of eigenvalues that stand outside the Marchenko-Pastur sea: the market mode, in which everything co-moves, and the sector modes. Random matrix theory is the spectral view of the same block structure that Bron-Kerbosch hunts for combinatorially. The connection runs deeper than an analogy. A correlated sector is a planted dense subgraph, and the planted-clique problem says such a block is detectable cheaply from the leading eigenvector once it is larger than about n\sqrt{n}n​, but is conjectured to be combinatorially hard below that size. Cleaning the matrix with random matrix theory first is therefore not just hygiene. It is the cheap spectral relaxation of the same NP-hard clique problem we then solve by brute force, and the gap between n\sqrt{n}n​ and log⁡n\log nlogn is this post’s exact-versus-approximate tension seen from the spectral side.

Takeaways

  • Diversification has a clean combinatorial core: a maximally diversified portfolio is a Maximum Clique in a correlation-threshold graph (equivalently a Maximum Independent Set in its complement).
  • That core is NP-hard, and the practical tension is the usual one. Exact enumeration gives portfolios large enough to matter but does not scale; the polynomial approximation scales but returns sets too small to diversify.
  • On a universe the size of the S&P 500, exhaustive Bron-Kerbosch is still the only thing that produces a portfolio worth holding.

This is the second of three problems from the dissertation. The others recast debt resolution and currency arbitrage as graph problems in the same way.

References

  • C. Bron and J. Kerbosch (1973). Algorithm 457: Finding All Cliques of an Undirected Graph. Communications of the ACM 16(9).
  • J. W. Moon and L. Moser (1965). On Cliques in Graphs. Israel Journal of Mathematics 3. Establishes the 3n/33^{n/3}3n/3 bound on maximal cliques.
  • R. Boppana and M. M. Halldórsson (1992). Approximating Maximum Independent Sets by Excluding Subgraphs. BIT 32(2).
  • M. Statman (1987). How Many Stocks Make a Diversified Portfolio? Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis 22(3).
  • L. Laloux, P. Cizeau, J.-P. Bouchaud, and M. Potters (1999). Noise Dressing of Financial Correlation Matrices. Physical Review Letters 83(7). Random matrix theory applied to the correlation matrix.
  • N. Alon, M. Krivelevich, and B. Sudakov (1998). Finding a Large Hidden Clique in a Random Graph. The spectral n\sqrt{n}n​ threshold for the planted-clique problem.

entry.title: The Complexity of Deterministic Arbitrage
entry.summary: Testing the efficient market hypothesis by searching foreign-exchange tick data for arbitrage. Currencies become a gain graph, arbitrage becomes a negative cycle, the search is NP-hard, and four methods (exhaustive enumeration, ant colony optimization, Bellman-Ford, and Karp’s minimum mean weight cycle) are benchmarked on 2008 and 2012 tick data.
entry.content:
Adapted from a chapter of my undergraduate dissertation, “Solving Financial Problems using Algorithmic Graph Theory” (2013).

  • The Problem
  • Currencies as a Gain Graph
    • Arbitrage is a negative cycle
  • Complexity
  • The Data
  • Four Ways to Find the Cycle
    • 1. Exhaustive enumeration (the reference)
    • 2. Ant colony optimization
    • 3. Bellman-Ford
    • 4. Minimum mean weight cycle
  • Results
  • Discussion
  • Takeaways
  • References

The Problem

Deterministic arbitrage is buying and selling the same security at different prices to pocket the difference. Whether such differences exist, and how large they are, is tied to market efficiency.

The law of one price. In an efficient market, all equal securities have only one price at a given time.

The efficient market hypothesis posits that all publicly available information and future expectations are reflected in an asset’s price, and as a corollary the law of one price holds. We test the hypothesis by looking for arbitrage in the foreign exchange market, and along the way ask what “processing all publicly available information” actually costs, once that information includes not just explicit prices but the implicit prices hidden in chains of trades.

Currencies as a Gain Graph

Currencies trade in pairs, so a currency that can be exchanged for many others has many prices. Starting from capital in currency c1c_1c1​, the explicit price of a desired currency c2c_2c2​ is just the exchange rate:

pc1,c2=r1,2. p_{c_1, c_2} = r_{1,2}. pc1​,c2​​=r1,2​.

An implicit price of a currency cnc_ncn​ is any price obtained by routing through intermediates c2…cn−1c_2 \ldots c_{n-1}c2​…cn−1​:

pc1,cn=r1,2 r2,3 ⋯ rn−1,n=∏i=1n−1ri,i+1. p_{c_1, c_n} = r_{1,2}\, r_{2,3}\, \cdots\, r_{n-1,n} = \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} r_{i,i+1}. pc1​,cn​​=r1,2​r2,3​⋯rn−1,n​=i=1∏n−1​ri,i+1​.

Model NNN currencies as a directed graph. The implicit price of a currency as a function of itself is any cycle in that graph, and a cycle whose implicit price is below 1 is an arbitrage opportunity. Define the gain of a trade as the factor by which capital grows, γ=1/(implicit price)\gamma = 1 / (\text{implicit price})γ=1/(implicit price).

This is a gain graph: a digraph whose edges carry an invertible gain γ∈R+\gamma \in \mathbb{R}^+γ∈R+, with the reverse edge carrying γ−1\gamma^{-1}γ−1. At a fixed exchange rate without fees the gain function f(x)=γxf(x) = \gamma xf(x)=γx is linear, which matters: a single series of trades (a path) always dominates any blend of trades (a flow), so we only ever need to look for a cycle. The gain of a cycle is the product of its edge gains,

gain=∏i=1…nγi, \text{gain} = \prod_{i=1\ldots n} \gamma_i, gain=i=1…n∏​γi​,

and an arbitrage exists whenever some cycle has gain >1> 1>1.

Arbitrage is a negative cycle

Taking logarithms turns the product into a sum and the search into a classic one. Weight each edge with −log⁡γi-\log \gamma_i−logγi​; then a gain above 1 is exactly a cycle of negative total weight:

∑i=1…n−log⁡γi<log⁡1=0. \sum_{i=1\ldots n} -\log \gamma_i < \log 1 = 0. i=1…n∑​−logγi​<log1=0.

Commissions slot straight in. If an exchange charges a percentage fee ϕ\phiϕ per transaction, the gain function f(x)=ϕ γ xf(x) = \phi\,\gamma\,xf(x)=ϕγx is still linear, so the same reasoning applies with one extra term per edge:

∑i=1…n−log⁡γi−log⁡ϕi<0. \sum_{i=1\ldots n} -\log \gamma_i - \log \phi_i < 0. i=1…n∑​−logγi​−logϕi​<0.

Complexity

Finding a gain-generating cycle is finding the longest path from a node back to itself (or, in the log-sum form, a shortest negative one). In a complete graph the number of distinct cycles is

cycle number=∑k=3∣V∣12(∣V∣k)(k−1)!, \text{cycle number} = \sum_{k=3}^{|V|} \frac{1}{2} \binom{|V|}{k} (k-1)!, cycle number=k=3∑∣V∣​21​(k∣V∣​)(k−1)!,

and a currency graph, tradable in both directions, has twice that. This grows far too fast to enumerate.

Deciding whether a graph contains a path of length at least kkk is NP-complete. It is in NP, since summing the edges of a candidate path verifies its length. It is NP-hard by reduction from the Hamilton Path problem: an efficient longest-path algorithm would answer whether the longest path in a unit-weighted graph has length ∣V∣−1|V| - 1∣V∣−1, i.e. whether a Hamilton path exists.

The same hardness rules out an exact online algorithm, one allowed a finite preparation step and then required to solve a stream of inputs i1…ini_1 \ldots i_ni1​…in​ in polynomial time each. If one existed, feeding it the graph one edge at a time would solve longest-path in polynomial time, contradicting the reduction. And the practical scale is unforgiving: the UN recognises 182 circulating currencies, far more than can be searched exhaustively. We need approximations, and a way to know how good they are.

The Data

To benchmark the methods we used foreign-exchange tick data from GAIN Capital. A tick is registered whenever the best bid or best ask improves on one of the observed exchanges; the smallest increment is a basis point, here 0.000010.000010.00001. Commissions for FX are quoted in basis points and can be as low as 0.10.10.1 bp (0.001%0.001\%0.001%); since currencies trade in lots of 100,000, fee thresholds are easily met, so the fee is modelled as a flat percentage per trade.

Markets are presumed less efficient under uncertainty, and a proxy for uncertainty is how fast prices move: volatility, scaled over a span as σT=σT\sigma_T = \sigma \sqrt{T}σT​=σT​. EUR/USD volatility over 2007-2013 peaks sharply in late 2008. We used two datasets accordingly: the last week of 2008 (13 currencies, maximum measured volatility) and the first week of 2012 (33 currencies). Prices are stepped through with an iterator that, for each moment ttt, holds the most recent bid and ask for every currency up to ttt; the algorithms run on the iterator at every sequence number in a dataset.

Four Ways to Find the Cycle

1. Exhaustive enumeration (the reference)

To know whether an approximation found the best cycle at a moment, we need the ground truth, so we precompute all cycles by backtracking. Since the graph is not complete (not every currency trades directly against every other) and the tradable pairs stay fixed across a week, the set of valid cycles is computed once. For the 2008 graph (13 currencies) this yields 1,454 cycles; for the 2012 graph (33 currencies) it is already infeasible, with far too many simple cycles to enumerate.

2. Ant colony optimization

The first approximation borrows from nature. Ants lay pheromone on the way back from food; shorter trips refresh pheromone faster, so good routes self-reinforce. Occasionally ants get trapped reinforcing a closed loop, an ant mill, where they circle until they die of exhaustion (a behaviour reported by Schneirla). Here that pathology is the goal: we want our digital ants stuck in a gain-generating cycle.

Following Dorigo, each of AAA ants starts at a random currency and walks the graph, using each currency at most once. The chance of moving from currency sss to currency iii blends the exchange-rate gain γ\gammaγ with the edge’s pheromone level ϕ\phiϕ:

psi=ϕ(i) γ(s,i)∑j≠iϕ(j) γ(s,j). p_{si} = \frac{\phi(i)\,\gamma(s, i)}{\sum_{j \neq i} \phi(j)\,\gamma(s, j)}. psi​=∑j=i​ϕ(j)γ(s,j)ϕ(i)γ(s,i)​.

When an ant returns to a currency it has already visited, a cycle is complete; its gain is added to the pheromone on each of its edges, the all-time best is saved, and after every iteration a fraction ϵ=0.02\epsilon = 0.02ϵ=0.02 of all pheromone evaporates so that stale routes fade. The whole thing runs for III iterations, giving complexity O(I A ∣V∣)\mathrm{O}(I\,A\,|V|)O(IA∣V∣).

Does the pheromone machinery actually help? Compared against the same walk with pheromone disabled, which is to say picking random cycles, ant colony optimization averaged a gain of μ=1.00071\mu = 1.00071μ=1.00071 over 21,004 trades, versus μ=1.00064\mu = 1.00064μ=1.00064 over 18,452 for random selection. With no clear distribution over the gain cycles, the difference is not significant. Sweeping the parameters, A=2∣V∣A = 2|V|A=2∣V∣ and I=4∣V∣I = 4|V|I=4∣V∣ gave the best results for the least time.

3. Bellman-Ford

Bellman-Ford computes shortest paths from a single source by relaxation, repeatedly improving the best known distance to each vertex:

function Relax(δ, π, γ):
    for u in V:
        for v in V − {u}:
            if δ[v] > δ[u] + γ(u, v):
                δ[v] ← δ[u] + γ(u, v)
                π[v] ← u

After ∣V∣−1|V| - 1∣V∣−1 relaxations every acyclic shortest path from the source has been found. The detection theorem is the lever:

If after ∣V∣−1|V| - 1∣V∣−1 relaxations a shortest path can still be improved by one more relaxation, the graph contains a negative cycle.

The proof is short: a shortest path can hold no non-negative cycle (deleting it cannot lengthen the path), so in a graph without negative cycles all shortest paths are acyclic and at most ∣V∣−1|V| - 1∣V∣−1 edges long, and ∣V∣−1|V| - 1∣V∣−1 relaxations suffice. If a further relaxation still improves some distance to vvv via uuu, then a negative cycle exists and the edge e(u,v)e(u, v)e(u,v) lies on it. We recover the cycle by walking the predecessor pointers π\piπ backwards until a node repeats. This gives a polynomial-time test for arbitrage, O(∣V∣ ∣E∣)\mathrm{O}(|V|\,|E|)O(∣V∣∣E∣), with one weakness: the particular cycle returned depends on the start vertex and relaxation order.

4. Minimum mean weight cycle

The last method finds the cycle of minimum mean weight, using Karp’s characterization. The mean weight of a kkk-edge cycle is μ=1k∑i=1kγi\mu = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \gamma_iμ=k1​∑i=1k​γi​. Add a source sss joined to every vertex at exchange rate 1 (weight log⁡1=0\log 1 = 0log1=0 in the log-sum form), and let δk(s,v)\delta_k(s, v)δk​(s,v) be the shortest path from sss to vvv using exactly kkk edges. Then the minimum mean cycle is

μ∗=min⁡v∈V  max⁡0≤k≤n−1  δn(s,v)−δk(s,v)n−k. \mu^* = \min_{v \in V}\; \max_{0 \le k \le n-1}\; \frac{\delta_n(s, v) - \delta_k(s, v)}{n - k}. μ∗=v∈Vmin​0≤k≤n−1max​n−kδn​(s,v)−δk​(s,v)​.

We compute the δk\delta_kδk​ table with a layered Bellman-Ford pass, store predecessors, and recover the cycle’s edges with a prefix-cost array WWW, the cumulative cost down the retrieved path, so that checking whether a subpath of the right length realises μ∗\mu^*μ∗ is a constant-time lookup W[k+l]−W[k]W[k+l] - W[k]W[k+l]−W[k].

The catch is that minimum mean weight is not maximum gain. The cycle with the best per-edge average is not the cycle with the best total return.

Results

Averaged over the whole 2008 dataset, Bellman-Ford is the fastest by a wide margin: it maintains two length-nnn vectors (δ\deltaδ and π\piπ), where the minimum mean cycle needs a full n×nn \times nn×n matrix.

Method Theoretical complexity Time (ms)
Precomputed cycles cycle number 0.4587
Bellman-Ford $\mathrm{O}( V
Minimum mean cycle $\mathrm{O}( V
Ant colony $\mathrm{O}(I\,A\, V

The arbitrage opportunities themselves are small and fleeting. The best opportunity over the whole week was γ=1.0064\gamma = 1.0064γ=1.0064, a 0.64%0.64\%0.64% gain. Gain-generating cycles up to length 11 turn up, but the longest cycle that is ever the best at a given moment is 7.

Method Opportunities Avg gain Avg length Longest Best cycle
Precomputed cycles 42,217 1.00076 3.19 7 1.006380
Bellman-Ford 42,217 1.00071 3.55 7 1.006172
Minimum mean cycle 42,018 1.00075 3.08 6 1.006380
Ant colony 15,480 1.00070 4.12 7 1.006058

Bellman-Ford finds an arbitrage at every one of the 42,217 moments the exhaustive reference does, confirming that if an opportunity exists in the graph, the negative-cycle test detects it. The minimum mean cycle method matches the very best cycle but visibly favours short ones, never finding a cycle longer than 6. Ant colony optimization detects far fewer opportunities and, as already noted, barely beats random search.

Discussion

If the data is correct, arbitrage opportunities do exist in foreign exchange, even after commissions. Strictly, each one means the prices are momentarily outside equilibrium, a small dent in the efficient market hypothesis. The more interesting result is the computational one: pricing all publicly available information, once “all information” includes implicit prices, is NP-hard. The law of one price can fail not because traders are irrational but because finding the mispricing is intractable in general. Good polynomial-time approximations exist, and Bellman-Ford in particular gives an exact, polynomial test for whether an arbitrage exists, even if the specific cycle it returns is arbitrary.

The caveats are real. GAIN Capital does not publish its sources, so the dataset likely covers only a slice of exchanges; latency between observer and exchange means the price seen at time ttt may not be the price on the exchange at ttt, and the same relativity bites when a trade is submitted and the market moves while the order travels. The natural next step is data with volume and full market depth at every price change, rather than top-of-book bid/ask alone.

A decade later. I would no longer call this a dent in the efficient market hypothesis. Opportunities this small and this short-lived, once latency and the bid-ask spread are accounted for, are roughly what a near-efficient market looks like. Efficiency is a limiting fiction, held in place by the very arbitrageurs who profit from violating it. Grossman and Stiglitz (1980) put the point sharply: a perfectly efficient market cannot exist, because then no one would be paid to gather the information that makes it efficient. My original reading, that these prices are technically outside equilibrium, I still hold. It simply points somewhere other than market efficiency. It points at markets as out-of-equilibrium systems, which is the direction my later work took.

The computational angle survives in a sharper form. The honest claim is not that markets are hard and therefore inefficient. It is that complexity creates asymmetry. Arora, Barak, Brunnermeier, and Ge (2011) show that structured products can hide junk that is NP-hard to detect, by reduction from densest subgraph, so intractability can let a seller sustain a mispricing that a buyer cannot efficiently uncover.

Takeaways

  • FX arbitrage is a negative-cycle problem: weight each rate by −log⁡γ-\log\gamma−logγ and an arbitrage is a negative-weight cycle, commissions included.
  • Searching for the best cycle is NP-hard (via Hamilton path), but detecting an arbitrage is polynomial: Bellman-Ford does it in O(∣V∣∣E∣)\mathrm{O}(|V||E|)O(∣V∣∣E∣) and was both the fastest and the most reliable method tested.
  • Nature-inspired search (ant colony) underwhelmed here, barely beating random cycles; Karp’s minimum mean weight cycle is elegant but optimises the wrong objective, preferring short cycles to high-gain ones.
  • Arbitrage opportunities were present even net of fees: tiny (≤0.64%\le 0.64\%≤0.64%) and short-lived, but real in this data.

This is the last of three problems from the dissertation. The others cast debt resolution and portfolio diversification as graph problems in the same spirit.

References

  • R. M. Karp (1978). A Characterization of the Minimum Cycle Mean in a Digraph. Discrete Mathematics 23(3).
  • M. Dorigo (1992). Optimization, Learning and Natural Algorithms. PhD thesis, Politecnico di Milano.
  • T. C. Schneirla (1944). A unique case of circular milling in ants. American Museum Novitates.
  • A. V. Goldberg and T. Radzik (1993). A heuristic improvement of the Bellman-Ford algorithm. Applied Mathematics Letters 6(3).
  • R. E. Bellman (1958), L. R. Ford Jr. The shortest-path relaxation algorithm.
  • S. J. Grossman and J. E. Stiglitz (1980). On the Impossibility of Informationally Efficient Markets. American Economic Review 70(3).
  • S. Arora, B. Barak, M. Brunnermeier, and R. Ge (2011). Computational Complexity and Information Asymmetry in Financial Products. Communications of the ACM 54(5).
